entities.description: A publicly viewable electronic text of Horstmann's article. It preserves the German introduction, serializes the Middle English translation and parallel Latin text as separate divisions, and retains printed page milestones, editorial notes, italics, and superscript expansions.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item CME00003
notes: The compact wrapper exposes no item-level statement identifying OCR versus manual keying or an original electronic-publication date.
A revision note dated 6-2-16 records structural validation, title-page proofing, page-break review, and conversion of the Latin comparison from notes into a version DIV1 with chapter milestones.
The 81 PB elements repeat print pages across the introduction, Middle English, and Latin divisions and do not represent 81 distinct manuscript surfaces.

rights.basis: The current item page expressly states that the materials are in the public domain and applies No Copyright-United States.
rights.component: Materials presented in U-M CME item CME00003
rights.jurisdiction: United States

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Informacio Alredi abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam: translata de latino in anglicum per Thomam N.,' Englische Studien 7 (1884), 304-344.
bibliographic.title: Informacio Alredi abbatis monasterij de Rieualle ad sororem suam inclusam: translata de latino in anglicum per Thomam N.

dateStatements.label: publication
dateStatements.notes: Horstmann's signature reads Berlin, October 1883. The issue 1 title page is dated 1883, while the completed-volume title page and standard article citations are dated 1884.

entities.description: The immediate print source for U-M: Horstmann's German introduction and Middle English transcription from Vernon fols. a-k, accompanied page by page by the Latin text then printed under pseudo-Augustine in Patrologia Latina 32. Horstmann notes uncertain abbreviation expansions and the different English and Latin chapter systems.
entities.label: Horstmann's 1884 edition of the Vernon translation
notes: The scan's issue 1 title page reads Heilbronn: Verlag von Gebr. Henninger, 1883, and its completed-volume title page repeats that place and publisher with the date 1884. BSB/DDB catalog metadata instead gives Leipzig: O. R. Reisland, 1884; the bibliographic fields follow the direct title-page evidence.

rights.basis: The 1884 article and 1857 Migne volume are far older than the 95-year maximum U.S. term described for pre-1978 protected works; U-M also labels its Horstmann-derived item public domain in the United States.
rights.component: Horstmann's 1884 transcription, introduction, notes, and the incorporated older Migne comparison text
rights.jurisdiction: United States

dateStatements.label: preliminary-quire assessment
dateStatements.notes: Ayto's older palaeographical discussion is retained separately from the current Bodleian whole-codex date.
dateStatements.value: about 1390, possibly extending into the early fifteenth century

entities.description: The sole known witness of this Middle English translation. It occupies ten pages in the manuscript's added preliminary quire, modern fols. IIIv-VIIIr; Horstmann's later page lettering calls the same span fols. a-k.

holding.notes: Digital Bodleian supplies the exact item record and a complete 710-canvas surrogate.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413, with 71 leaves thought lost. The work occupies fols. IIIv-VIIIr.
physicalDescription.format: Large religious and didactic miscellany, normally in gatherings of eight
notes: The codex contains more than 350 items and is written in double and triple columns; the work's preliminary-quire pages use three columns.
The current item description gives England, West Midlands? as origin and 1381-1400 as its date statement.
Ayto describes the work quire's hand as Anglicana Formata and its surface language as localized between Worcester and Birmingham.

rights.basis: The source work and translation are medieval; no copyright term persists in their historical wording. This does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, or photographs.
rights.component: The medieval Middle English translation and Aelred's underlying Latin work
rights.jurisdiction: Worldwide copyright-duration assessment

entities.description: Aelred's Latin rule for his enclosed sister. The Vernon translation renders only its final twenty sections, which concern inner spiritual life, personal morality, prayer, and meditation; it omits the opening practical directions for external anchoritic observance.
entities.label: Aelred of Rievaulx, De institutione inclusarum
notes: This entity represents the source work, not a claim that Thomas N. used any identified surviving Latin manuscript or printed edition.

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is the partial Middle English Vernon translation, not Aelred's complete Latin treatise, Horstmann's German introduction or parallel Latin text, the distinct Bodley 423 translation, or the whole Vernon miscellany.
The official Bodleian IIIF range fixes the work at modern fols. IIIv-VIIIr and canvases 10-19; visual inspection confirms the opening rubric and final Amen.

The complete 710-canvas physical codex was cached rather than only the ten target canvases. The bundle's 1600-pixel image requests preserve every native-size fallback URL in its inventory; complete means complete surface coverage, not preservation-master resolution.
Horstmann's full journal volume was likewise cached as a complete 524-canvas bundle; his article occupies printed pp. 304-344, IIIF canvases 320-360.
Bodleian image licensing, BSB scan terms, the medieval text, old and modern editions, U-M encoding, Durham thesis, and local XML are separate rights layers.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the CME-submodule XML for University of Michigan item CME00003. U-M encoded Carl Horstmann's first edition in Englische Studien 7 (1884), pp. 304-344: a German introduction, the nineteen-chapter Middle English Vernon translation, and an older parallel Latin text. Horstmann transcribed the Middle English work from the Vernon Manuscript, now Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, where it occupies fols. IIIv-VIIIr (official IIIF canvases 10-19); his legacy page letters a-k refer to the same ten manuscript pages. The translation, attributed in its medieval rubric to Thomas N., renders only the final twenty sections of Aelred of Rievaulx's De institutione inclusarum. A complete 710-canvas color surrogate of the physical Vernon codex and a complete 524-canvas scan of the 1884 journal volume have been cached as auditable IIIF bundles, with the work mapped inside each full object. Ayto's 1973 manuscript-based thesis is openly downloadable; the 1984 Ayto-Barratt EETS edition is represented as a later comparison source available through purchase and controlled borrowing, not as an ancestor of the U-M text.

catalogSummary.author: Thomas N. (translator of Aelred of Rievaulx; identity uncertain)
catalogSummary.date: Fourteenth century; necessarily before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400
catalogSummary.region: Translation region unknown; the surviving copy has a West Midland surface layer over mixed earlier dialect strata
catalogSummary.title: De institutione inclusarum (Vernon translation)

catalogingSubject.label: De institutione inclusarum (Vernon translation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The partial fourteenth-century Middle English translation of Aelred of Rievaulx's De institutione inclusarum preserved on fols. IIIv-VIIIr of the Vernon Manuscript and attributed by its rubric to Thomas N. The subject excludes Aelred's complete Latin work, the distinct Middle English translation in Bodley 423, the physical Vernon codex, Horstmann's 1884 edition and parallel Latin text, the U-M electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Ascetic guide
contentParts.summary: Chapters 1-13 counsel the recluse on chastity, resistance to temptation, bodily discipline, humility, charity, and the cultivation of virtues.

contentParts.label: Private prayer and meditation
contentParts.summary: Chapters 14-19 cultivate love of God through meditations on Christ's life and passion, present grace, death, judgment, and future blessedness.

assertion.notes: The surviving codex supplies a terminus ante quem rather than a precise translation date. Ayto argues that multiple earlier linguistic layers probably push translation appreciably earlier than the surviving copy, but gives no secure year.

dateStatements.display: Fourteenth century; before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400
Vernon Manuscript, 1381-1400 (thought to date from c. 1390)

scope.description: The sole known witness of this particular translation

notes: Horstmann signed the article 'Berlin, Oct. 1883'; the journal volume was published in 1884.

scope.description: Horstmann's first printed edition in Englische Studien 7

extent.description: The cataloged Middle English translation has nineteen prose chapters in two major intellectual divisions. The complete XML artifact additionally contains Horstmann's two-paragraph German introduction and a separately serialized parallel Latin text with twenty DIV2 elements (nineteen chapter groups plus an epilogue). Within the Middle English DIV1 the XML contains 19 P, 39 PB, 236 MILESTONE, 54 NOTE, 1,160 HI, and 132 SUP elements; these are encoding metrics, not intrinsic work counts.

formStatements.description: Continuous devotional and didactic prose divided into nineteen chapters. Brief Latin quotations and the two Latin verses printed after Horstmann's comparison text do not make the Middle English work mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The complete Latin work is an anchoritic rule, but this translation omits its practical opening section. Ayto therefore describes the surviving selection more strictly as general personal morality and private meditation.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The complete parallel Latin text in Horstmann and the XML is edition-level comparison material, not a second primary language of the cataloged Middle English work.

scope.description: Rubrics, biblical citations, and other phrases embedded in the Middle English translation

notes: Horstmann's fols. a-k are a later page-lettering of the ten manuscript pages bearing the work; the current Bodleian surrogate identifies the same span as fols. IIIv-VIIIr.
The official Bodleian range and visual inspection establish IIIF canvases 10-19 as the exact work portion within the 710-canvas complete codex.
Rights for the medieval text, Horstmann edition, U-M encoding, Bodleian images, BSB scan, Ayto thesis, and 1984 EETS edition remain separate in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Review the full 1984 Ayto-Barratt introduction and scholarship published after it for any reassessment of Alexandra Barratt's Thomas Netter proposal.
Retain Thomas N. as the rubric's attribution without expanding N. to Netter or another surname absent decisive evidence.
openQuestions.question: Who was the translator named as Thomas N. in the Vernon rubric?

next_steps: Consult later linguistic work using the Linguistic Atlas of Late Mediaeval English and compare it with Ayto's stratified-language analysis.
Keep translation origin distinct from the West Midland surface language and origin of the Vernon copy.
openQuestions.question: Can the translation's date and original dialect region be established more narrowly than fourteenth century and unknown?

next_steps: Seek item-level U-M production metadata or a raw distribution package for CME00003.
Do not infer OCR, manual keying, or full correction from the present compact XML wrapper.
openQuestions.question: What production process and correction level created the U-M encoding now represented by CME00003.xml?

assertion.notes: Ayto identifies South-Eastern, East Midland, and probably Northern strata beneath the West Midland surface layer and says the original translation region cannot profitably be inferred.

notes: Do not convert the witness's West Midland copying context into a work-level place of composition.

scope.description: Surface language of the sole surviving Vernon copy, not a secure place of translation

notes: Aelred authored the Latin source, not the Middle English wording.

scope.description: Author of the Latin source passages translated by this Middle English version

assertion.notes: The rubric securely supplies the form Thomas N.; it does not securely identify the person behind that form.

scope.description: Middle English rendering of the final twenty sections of Aelred's Latin treatise
Horstmann's 1884 transcription, notes, and parallel Latin presentation, the immediate source of the U-M encoding

subjects.label: Chastity, virginity, humility, charity, and other virtues
Private prayer and meditation on past, present, and future things

summaries.value: A partial Middle English translation of Aelred of Rievaulx's rule for his enclosed sister. It omits the Latin work's practical opening directions and instead presents nineteen chapters on inner religious life: chastity and temptation, bodily discipline, humility, charity, prayer, imaginative meditation on Christ's life and passion, grace, death, judgment, and heavenly blessedness.

CME00003.xml serializes more than the medieval work: Horstmann's German introduction and the Latin comparison text are distinct edition-level components. The parallel Latin occupies the bottoms of Horstmann's printed pages but follows the English as a separate DIV1 in XML.

notes: The parenthetical qualifier distinguishes this incomplete translation from the separate, fuller Middle English version in Bodley 423.
This is the rubric visible at the opening of fol. IIIv; capitalization and punctuation follow Horstmann's presentation.

titles.value: Nou let heere here and vnderstonde ententyflyche myne wordes, what-euere heo beo that hath fursake this wordle and ychose solytarye lyf

work.author: Thomas N. (translator of Aelred of Rievaulx; identity uncertain)
work.dateDisplay: Fourteenth century; necessarily before the Vernon copy of 1381-1400
